content:
In the original sentence, the phrase "after finishing my presentation" is a dangling modifier because it is not logically or grammatically connected to the subject of the main clause, "the applause." Adding the subject "I" to the sentence clarifies that the speaker finished the presentation, leading to applause filling the room.

A better sentence would be: After I finished my presentation, applause filled the room.

After you finish this page, click Next to continue to Specifics of Grammar: Run-On Sentences.


Week 4 | Specifics of Grammar: Run-On Sentences

Run-On Sentences
A run-on sentence occurs when two or more independent clauses are joined without proper punctuation or conjunction. This results in a sentence that could be clearer to read and understand.

Two main ways to fix a run-on sentence are using proper punctuation or adding a conjunction and a comma. Proper punctuation means separating the independent clauses using a period, semicolon, or comma. It is important to note that the comma is only appropriate when the conjunction is used. Using a comma alone would transform the run-on sentence into a comma spice, another common error to avoid. Adding a conjunction means joining the independent clauses together with a word like "and," "but," or "or." You might recall the popular acronym, FANBOYS, which stands for For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
